content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 9, 2001


Michael Jenkins, City Attorney
City of Diamond Bar
Jenkins & Hogin, LLP
1325 Nineteenth Street
Manhattan Beach, CA 90266

Re:   Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-213

Dear Mr. Jenkins:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Diamond Bar regarding the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts as they have been presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

     May a monthly newsletter, published by the City of Diamond Bar, be combined into a monthly publication produced by the Diamond Bar Chamber of Commerce?

CONCLUSION

     If the chamber's monthly newspaper includes photographs and stories about members of the Diamond Bar City Council anywhere in the newspaper, the newspaper would be a prohibited mass mailing under section 89001 if the city provides more than $50.

FACTS

     In an effort to increase communication with its residents, the City of Diamond Bar proposes to publish and mail a monthly newsletter to all property addresses in the city. The content of the city's newsletter would in all respects comply with the FPPC's mass mailing regulations.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-213
Page No. 2

The newsletter is proposed to be integrated into the monthly newspaper published
by the Chamber of Commerce. The chamber's newspaper is printed on newsprint,
tabloid newspaper style. As proposed, the integrated publication would retain the tabloid
format; the chamber portion would start on one side, and the city newsletter portion
would start on the other side, upside down (so that each can be read from left to right).
The publication would, in effect, have two cover pages. The two organizations' content
will be kept completely separate, articles will not be commingled on the same pages and
neither organization will have any editorial involvement in the other's portion of the
publication. The page layout and design will make it clear to the reader whether they are
reading the chamber newspaper or the city newsletter; indeed, a reader reading the
chamber portion would have to turn the publication upside down in order to read the city
newsletter (and vice versa). The sole purpose of this proposal is to increase readership,
avoid duplication of effort and spare residents from receiving multiple community-
oriented publications.

Unlike the city newsletter, the chamber's newspaper historically has included
content that would be prohibited by the mass mailing regulations, including occasional
pictures of incumbent council members, a monthly "op-ed" piece authored by the mayor
(whose name and picture appear aside the piece), and candidate statements of city council
candidates (including incumbents). The chamber portion of the proposed joint
publication would continue to include this type of content.

The city would pay the entire cost of its portion of the publication, including
distribution costs, which cost would exceed the dollar threshold in the mass mailing
regulations. The chamber would pay cost of its portion of the publication. No city funds
would be used in any manner towards the chamber portion of the publication.

ANALYSIS

Section 89001 provides that "[n]o newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent
at public expense." Regulation 18901 implements section 89001 and provides that a
mailing is prohibited if all of the following apply:

"(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the
recipient at his or her residence, place of employment or
business, or post office box. For purposes of this
subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient must
be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or
a written document.

"(2) The item sent either:

"(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the
agency which produces or sends the mailing, or

"(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other
reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency
which produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or

File No. A-01-213
Page No. 3

sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with the elected officer.

"(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with
public moneys; or

"(B) Costs of design, production, and printing
exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and the
design, production, or printing is done with the intent of
sending the item other than as permitted by this regulation.

"(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items
are sent, in a single calendar month, excluding any item
sent in response to an unsolicited request and any item
described in subdivision (b)." (Regulation 18901(a).)

Section 89001 prohibits a mailing that meets the criteria set forth above.

Definition of Mass Mailing and Unsolicited Requests

Regulation 18901 only applies to a "mass mailing" as defined by the Act. "Mass
mailing" means more than 200 substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not include
mail which is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry, or any
items that fall within the exceptions set forth in regulation 18901(b). (Regulation
18901(a)(4).)

Regulation 18901(c) provides that "[a] person who subscribes to newspapers or
other periodicals published by persons other than elected officers shall be deemed to have
made unsolicited requests for materials published in those subscription publications."
(Regulation 18901(c)(4)(E).) Pursuant to this provision, we have advised that a notice of
a public hearing published in a newspaper of general circulation that is delivered to
subscribers is not a mass mailing. (Valenti Advice Letter, No. I-91-264.) Similarly, we
have advised that the mass mailing prohibition does not apply to advertisements in
newspapers that are delivered to subscribers. (Geiogue Advice Letter, No. A-91-193.)

The chamber's monthly newspaper, as well as the city's, is delivered to every
household and merchant in the city. As such, the newspaper does not constitute a
subscription publication. Assuming that more than 200 monthly newspapers are
distributed, the chamber's and the city's monthly newspaper would be a "mass mailing"
for purposes of the prohibition.

Public Moneys

Regulation 18901 specifies that a mass mailing is sent at public expense within
the meaning of section 89001 if the costs of distribution are paid for with public moneys,
or more than $50 in public moneys is used to pay for the costs of design, production, and
printing, and such activity is done with the intent to send the item other than as permitted
by the regulation. (Regulation 18901(a)(3).)

File No. A-01-213
Page No. 4

We have advised that a mailing sent by a chamber of commerce is not sent at
public expense for purposes of section 89001 merely because the chamber receives some
public moneys from a public entity, unless the public entity expressly funds the
production and distribution of the mailing sent by the chamber. (Curtis Advice Letter,
No. A-94-053; Warner Advice Letter, No. I-88-392.) We have also advised that a
newsletter that is circulated and produced in part with public funds must comply with
section 89001. (Battersby Advice Letter, No. A-94-303; Moore Advice Letter, No. A-99-
234.) Under your facts, the city expressly funds the production and distribution of its half
of the newspaper distributed by the chamber. The newsletter, as proposed, is a single
document, partially funded by the city with public funds. Therefore, if the amount of
funding from the city is greater than $50 per edition, which you indicate to be the case in
your request for advice, the public funding requirement is met. (Vollbrecht Advice
Letter, No. A-97-155; Campbell Advice Letter, No. A-97-188.)

Inclusion of a Reference to an Elected Officer

If the item features an elected officer, or includes the name, photograph, or any
reference to an elected officer who is affiliated with the agency that produces or
distributes the mailing, the item is subject to the restrictions of Regulation 18901.
(Regulation 18901(a)(2).) The inclusion in the chamber's newspaper of elected officials
affiliated with the City of Diamond Bar (i.e., the name or photograph of the official)
would meet this requirement, as would a monthly "op-ed" piece authored by the mayor,
and city council candidate statements.

Having determined that the newsletter will meet all elements set forth in Section
89001, we advise that the newsletter, as proposed and if sent, would be a mass mailing
prohibited by the Act.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By:    Jody Feldman, Counsel
       Legal Division
